narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates an 80% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 2, Year 3 decile of 1, middle family income, LSAT score of 26, undergraduate GPA of 2.1, male gender, Asian racial background, and plans for part-time work. Typical students in our dataset have much stronger academics—like Year 1 and 3 deciles around 5.5-5.7, LSAT scores near 37, and GPAs over 3.2—putting you well below those benchmarks in key performance areas. The model weighed these factors to make its call, with your LSAT score having the strongest influence, pushing heavily toward a failure prediction since it's so much lower than average. Next in impact was your Year 1 decile ranking, which also strongly signaled higher risk of failing due to its low position compared to peers. Your Year 3 decile followed closely, reinforcing that concern with an even lower standing that deviated sharply from the norm. Family income came after, with your middle bracket offering little buffer against the academic weaknesses. Your racial background as Asian had a moderate pull toward failure in this context, as it's less common than the typical White majority (84%) in successful cases. Undergraduate GPA and part-time work plans each nudged the prediction further toward risk, as they're below typical full-time, higher-achieving profiles. Only your gender as male provided a slight positive offset, gently pushing toward success since males are slightly more common (56%) among passers. Overall, these stacked up to tip the scales against you. To boost your future bar exam odds, focus on retaking the LSAT to aim closer to 37 or above, commit to full-time study instead of part-time to match the 93% of successful students, and build stronger academic habits early to lift future grade deciles. With targeted improvements there, you could shift your profile toward the passing patterns we see in approved applicants. Keep pushing—you've got the drive to turn this around.
